policy.title: RBI finalizes climate scenario analysis requirements for scheduled commercial banks
policy.description: RBI issued final guidelines requiring all scheduled commercial banks to conduct climate scenario analysis by March 2027, with interim stress testing submissions due by September 2026. Banks with assets over ₹10 lakh crore must incorporate physical risk adjustments into PD/LGD models within 18 months.

policy.title: SEBI extends BRSR Core assurance mandate to top 500 listed companies
policy.description: SEBI expanded mandatory reasonable assurance requirements for BRSR Core metrics to top 500 companies by market capitalization, effective FY2026-27. The regulator also introduced standardized climate transition plan disclosures aligned with ISSB S2.

policy.title: IRDAI releases sandbox guidelines for climate parametric products
policy.description: IRDAI announced a regulatory sandbox for parametric insurance products covering cyclone, flood, and drought perils, with expedited 45-day approval for bank-insurer partnerships. Maximum sum insured raised to ₹50 crore for commercial parametric covers.

policy.title: Basel Committee finalizes Pillar 3 climate risk disclosure framework
policy.description: BCBS published final Pillar 3 disclosure requirements for climate-related financial risks, mandating quantitative metrics on physical and transition risk exposures starting January 2028. Includes standardized templates for geographic concentration of climate-sensitive assets.

policy.title: ISSB releases implementation guidance for emerging market jurisdictions
policy.description: IFRS Foundation published jurisdiction-specific guidance for ISSB S1/S2 adoption in emerging markets, including proportionality provisions and phased timelines for scope 3 emissions. India cited as a priority jurisdiction for 2026-27 adoption support.

policy.title: NGFS releases Phase V climate scenarios with enhanced physical risk pathways
policy.description: NGFS published updated climate scenarios incorporating latest IPCC AR6 damage functions and regional physical risk granularity for South Asia. New scenarios include 1.5°C overshoot pathway with acute event intensification factors.
policy.source: Network for Greening the Financial System

competitors.title: Jupiter Intelligence partners with HDFC Bank for India physical risk assessment
competitors.description: Jupiter announced a strategic partnership with HDFC Bank to provide portfolio-level physical risk scoring across 12 climate perils for the bank's commercial lending book. Deployment covers 15,000+ corporate borrower locations across India.

competitors.title: Sprih raises $18M Series B to expand climate risk modules for Indian banks
competitors.description: Bangalore-based Sprih closed $18M Series B led by Lightspeed India, with participation from existing investor Sequoia Surge. Funds will accelerate development of physical risk analytics and RBI-compliant climate reporting tools.

competitors.title: Moody's launches India-specific climate-adjusted credit risk module
competitors.description: Moody's ESG Solutions released a climate overlay for Indian corporate credit assessments, incorporating district-level physical hazard data and transition pathway analysis. Product integrates directly with Moody's credit ratings workflow.

competitors.title: XDI releases asset-level physical risk API with India coverage expansion
competitors.description: Cross Dependency Initiative expanded its physical risk API to cover 2.3 million asset locations in India, including granular flood, cyclone, and heat stress projections to 2100. API offers RBI-aligned risk classification outputs.

competitors.title: GIST Advisory partners with CRISIL for natural capital valuation in credit ratings
competitors.description: GIST Advisory announced integration of its natural capital accounting datasets into CRISIL's ESG rating methodology. Partnership covers 500+ Indian corporates with biodiversity and water dependency scoring.

competitors.title: Watershed launches financial services module with loan-level emissions attribution
competitors.description: Watershed released a dedicated banking module enabling financed emissions calculation at individual loan level, with PCAF-aligned methodology and integration with core banking systems. Initial pilots with three global banks announced.

research.title: Climate Risk and Bank Lending: Evidence from Emerging Markets
research.description: Study of 450 banks across 28 emerging markets finds that physical climate risk exposure correlates with 15-40 basis point increases in NPL ratios. Indian banks showed highest sensitivity to flood risk among South Asian peers.

research.title: Parametric Insurance for Climate Resilience in South Asia
research.description: Report estimates $8.2 billion annual protection gap for climate perils in India, with parametric products suited for 40% of this gap. Cyclone and flood parametrics identified as highest-viability products for bank distribution channels.

research.title: Physical Risk Transmission to Bank Credit Portfolios
research.description: BIS working paper quantifies physical risk transmission channels, finding that 1-standard-deviation increase in flood exposure raises corporate default probability by 8.3% within 24 months. Proposes standardized PD adjustment methodology for supervisory stress tests.

research.title: ESG Data Gaps in Emerging Markets: A Landscape Assessment
research.description: IFC assessment finds that only 23% of listed companies in South Asia provide decision-useful climate data, versus 67% in developed markets. Identifies physical risk exposure, supply chain emissions, and water stress as critical data gaps for investors.

research.title: Climate Scenario Analysis: Lessons from Early Adopters
research.description: Analysis of 35 banks' climate scenario exercises reveals that physical risk integration into credit models remains nascent, with only 12% of banks applying asset-level hazard data. Recommends granular geospatial approaches over sectoral proxies.

research.title: Climate Insurance Protection Gap: India Country Assessment
research.description: Munich Re estimates India's climate insurance penetration at 1.1% of economic losses, with agricultural and SME segments most underserved. Identifies index-based flood and cyclone covers as scalable solutions with 3-5 year payback for product development.

ourRead.headline: RBI's September 2026 stress testing deadline creates an urgent procurement window for climate risk analytics — StepChange must move fast before global players cement bank relationships.
body: This month's signals converge on a single theme: Indian financial regulators are done with voluntary approaches, and banks now face hard deadlines for climate risk integration. The RBI's finalized guidelines, SEBI's BRSR expansion, and IRDAI's parametric sandbox collectively signal that climate finance infrastructure is moving from pilot to production across the Indian financial system.

The RBI's March 28 directive is the most consequential policy development for StepChange in the past quarter. The September 2026 interim submission deadline — just five months away — creates immediate demand for physical risk data that can feed into PD/LGD adjustments. The ₹10 lakh crore threshold means India's top 15-20 banks are now on the clock. StepChange's core proposition of translating hazard data into credit risk metrics is precisely what these banks need, but only if we can demonstrate RBI-alignment in our methodology documentation.

The competitor signals demand attention: Jupiter's HDFC partnership and Moody's India-specific climate credit module show that global players are no longer treating India as a future market — they're here now. Sprih's $18M raise indicates domestic competition is also scaling. However, the Jupiter-HDFC deal likely took 12+ months to close, meaning banks making decisions today are still in play. StepChange's advantage lies in combining global-quality analytics with local regulatory fluency and faster deployment — but we must prove this in the next 60-90 days.

The BIS working paper on physical risk transmission provides methodological validation StepChange should leverage immediately. Their finding that flood exposure increases default probability by 8.3% within 24 months — with a proposed standardized PD adjustment methodology — gives us an academically credible framework to anchor our models. We should explicitly align our documentation to BIS recommendations and cite this paper in bank conversations. Similarly, the IFC data gaps report reinforces our Global South data thesis: the 23% vs 67% disclosure gap is exactly the market failure StepChange's proprietary datasets address.

One thing to watch: whether HDFC's Jupiter partnership triggers fast-follower behavior among peer banks or creates a wait-and-see dynamic. One action for this week: prepare a two-page brief showing how StepChange's PD adjustment methodology maps to both the RBI framework and the new BIS working paper — this becomes our qualification document for every bank conversation in Q2.
